m1-scaffold.notes:
Foundation of the product (the "Brain" app) is built and runs locally against the database; logging into the CMS admin works.

Done: one Next.js + Payload CMS application; single database connection; starting tables created; admin page loads; first account created.

Pending: nothing blocking at this stage. Note: live code-reload does not work on the shared S: network drive, so we run the app with the build+start commands — a dev-workflow detail only, no effect on the product.

m1-config-seam.notes:
One safe, validated place for all secret settings (database connection, signing key, AI key).

Done: a single audited config module; the app refuses to start with a clear message if a required setting is missing; secrets can never leak into a website visitor’s browser; 7 automated tests pass; full build verified.

Pending: an automatic lint check to prevent accidental misuse is deferred to the CI/testing task (m1-ci); the Gemini AI key is wired in when the chat agent is built.

m2-tenants.notes:
The list of customers (Tenants) the platform serves — one row per customer site.

Done: tenants table with name, unique slug, status, and slots for the GitHub repo and hosting targets that real onboarding will fill in later.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m2-changesets.notes:
The "ChangeSet" — the unit that ties a batch of edits together and tracks it all the way to going live. The full set of states is in place from the start.

Done: changesets table with the complete state machine, plus the key safety rule enforced by the database itself — a customer site can only have ONE in-progress batch of edits at a time (verified: the database rejects a second one). This is the hardened rule from the plan review.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m2-payload-collections.notes:
The content area — where a customer page's content lives, kept separate per customer and per batch of edits.

Done: a Pages collection with draft/published versions; every row is tied to a Tenant and a ChangeSet. Kept intentionally small for now (page title + hero heading/subheading); the richer page-builder blocks arrive with the layout features later.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m4-drafts.notes:
Draft vs. published content.

Done: pages keep separate draft and published versions, so edits pile up as drafts until the customer publishes.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m2-migrations.notes:
One clean, ordered history of database changes.

Done: a single migration created and applied for all the new tables, with the custom one-in-progress-ChangeSet safety rule included in that same history. Database changes always go through migrations, never by hand.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m2-fk-constraints.notes:
Database-level rule that no content can exist without an owner.

Done: ChangeSets require a Tenant (enforced); and as of 2026-06-20, Pages also require BOTH a Tenant and a ChangeSet at the database level (NOT NULL on pages.tenant_id + change_set_id_id, via migration pages_not_null) — verified the seed and structural-edit write paths still work. The app layer (ChangeSet hook + audited adapter) was already enforcing this; the DB constraint is the schema-layer backstop.

Pending: the FK ON DELETE policy review (RESTRICT vs SET NULL) so a Tenant/ChangeSet can't be deleted out from under its content.

m4-multitenant.notes:
Per-customer data separation (multi-tenancy).

Done: enabled the multi-tenant system so each customer's content is scoped to them; added an operator/super-admin flag for the platform owner; the database now has the per-customer link tables. App builds and runs cleanly.

Pending: the rule that forces every edit into a tracked batch, plus a small seed that creates a first test customer, come in the next step — that is when this becomes hands-on testable.

m4-beforechange.notes:
The rule that every content edit must belong to a tracked batch of changes.

Done: a shared hook now automatically tags every page edit with the customer's current open batch, and flatly rejects any edit when there is no open batch. Proven by automated tests (auto-tagged when a batch is open; rejected when none).

Pending: automatically opening a batch on the customer's very first edit (so they never hit a rejection) is handled in the next task, the safe write path.

m4-system-deny.notes:
Background/system processes are blocked from quietly changing customer content.

Done: only a small, explicitly-named set of internal purposes may write content; everything else is rejected. Proven by an automated test (a disallowed internal write is refused).

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m5-localapi-adapter.notes:
The single safe doorway through which the AI/agent changes content.

Done: one audited module is the only place that writes content; it always acts as the customer's dedicated machine account, with full per-customer security checks on. A cross-customer write hole (a customer's account could write into another customer's content via the raw database API) was caught by the new isolation tests and closed.

Pending: an extra concurrency safeguard (a per-site lock) is added later alongside the Discard feature where it is first needed; and an automatic check that nothing else in the codebase bypasses this doorway (next task).

m4-ensure-changeset.notes:
Automatically opening a batch of changes on the customer's first edit.

Done: the safe doorway opens a fresh batch when a customer makes their first edit (so they never hit a 'no batch open' error) and cleans up an empty batch if that first edit fails. Proven by an automated test.

Pending: wrapping open-and-write into one all-or-nothing database step (added with the concurrency safeguard later).

m5-isolation-tests.notes:
Automated proof that customers' data stays separated.

Done: tests confirm a customer's machine account can write its own content, is blocked from writing another customer's content, sees none of another customer's content, and auto-opens a batch on first write. These tests already caught and forced the fix of a real cross-customer hole.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m5-allowlist.notes:
A strict, locked-down format for the AI's edit instructions.

Done: the AI must return its requested change in one exact shape (which page, which field, the new text); anything unexpected (extra fields, unknown actions, wrong types) is rejected rather than guessed at. Proven by automated tests. This is the main safety check on the AI's output.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m6-model-seam.notes:
One config point for which AI model is used; set to OpenRouter (kimi-k2.6, falling back to qwen3.7-plus).

Done: the model client and fallback chain are built and proven live — the AI was called successfully and produced a valid edit.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m6-content-agent.notes:
The chat agent that turns a request like 'change the hero to X' into a real content edit.

Done: proven end-to-end — asked to change the hero heading to 'Summer Sale', the AI produced a valid instruction and the page draft updated through the safe doorway. Honest 'nothing was changed' on any failure.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m6-orchestrator.notes:
The agent only ever asks the safe doorway to make changes; it never touches the database directly.

Done: the agent reads and writes exclusively through the broker; verified by the working end-to-end edit.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m13-workspace.notes:
The customer's main screen: chat on the left, live preview on the right.

Done: a working chat-and-preview workspace — type a change in plain English, the AI applies it, and the preview updates. Includes a simple sign-in.

Pending: the 'Change layout' mode and a discard/start-over button come later (slice 1 is content editing only).

m8-inbrain-preview.notes:
The live preview pane that shows the customer's draft.

Done: the preview renders the draft page, with the customer's site determined securely from their sign-in (never from anything the browser sends) and only safe public fields shown. Refreshes after each edit.

Pending: the full separate-hosting preview (on Cloudflare) is a later module; this in-app preview stands in for now.

m11-tenant-auth.notes:
Customer sign-in that scopes them to their own site.

Done: signing in gates the workspace and ties the customer to their own content; the same login works for the admin. The site is derived from the session, not from anything the browser sends.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m5-lint-deny.notes:
An automatic guardrail so nothing in the codebase can bypass the single safe content-write doorway.

Done: a lint rule blocks any code from opening its own database connection — it must go through the audited broker; the framework's own files and tests are exempted. Lint passes with zero errors.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m13-direct-editor.notes:
Direct, on-canvas editing in the workspace (the no-AI path).

Done: click-to-edit text on every section; per-section background images and per-item (product) images, set by clicking the image right where it goes, with one-click revert/remove; full structural control on the canvas (move up/down, delete, add section from a picker, add/remove items); a 'Point AI here' button + Alt+E shortcut that targets a section for the AI so customers need not know section names; one-level Undo for the whole page; multi-page sites with a page switcher, route/address bar, and nav menu.

Pending: the embedded element-scoped Payload-admin editor panel proper (this is the lighter in-preview editor); richer section types (gallery/pricing).

m4-admin-scoped.notes:
The built-in CMS admin is scoped per customer.

Done: each customer signing into the admin sees and edits only their own content (enforced by the multi-tenant system); it is the direct-CMS fallback alongside the in-workspace click-to-edit.

Pending: nothing for this stage.

m6-structural-agent.notes:
The AI can build and fill whole sections from one request (the 'AI does design').

Done: a customer can say 'add a features section about our coffee shop' and the AI turns the section on and writes the heading and all three columns in one go — verified end-to-end (one sentence produced 8 coordinated edits). Works with text and with an attached reference image. Uses a fixed, safe set of sections (hero, features, call-to-action), each change still passing the same strict safety checks.

Pending: the fully-dynamic 'any building block in any order' version with a formal section library/registry is a later refinement.

m7-library.notes:
The library of reusable section building-blocks the AI composes pages from.

Done: seven section types — hero, features, PRODUCT CARDS (image, name, price, struck-through old price, discount badge, button), testimonials, call-to-action, contact, and text — each holding any number of items, composed in any order by the AI or by hand, with per-section and per-item images. Adding a new type is a quick platform change that then appears for every customer (proven by adding Products). Verified the AI composes Products correctly.

Pending: the formal machine-readable registry + CI validation + deployed-site (Astro) versions, plus a gallery type, are the deeper architecture for later.

m13-discard-ux.notes:
Undo / start-over.

Done: one-level page Undo (revert the last change; press again to redo), backed by a previousLayout snapshot taken on every write path (AI, text, image, structure).

Pending: the full Discard-the-whole-ChangeSet-back-to-Published transaction (quiesce in-flight writes, tear down branch/preview/staged media) lands with the Publish phase.

m13-publish-ux.notes:
Publish button → take the site live.

Done (2026-06-20): a green Publish button freezes the current drafts as the published version, renders the site to a self-contained static website, and (when Cloudflare is configured) deploys it to the customer's own Cloudflare Pages project via direct upload. The public URL is saved on the customer record and shown persistently in the workspace header. Falls back to a local /site/<slug> preview when Cloudflare isn't set. Verified live end-to-end (a real *.pages.dev site).

Pending: explicit Publishing/Reviewing states, locking edits during publish, and an honest pre-live vs post-deploy failure UX — these arrive with the full publish saga (Module 9).

m3-deploy-targets.notes:
Cloudflare deploy target.

Done (2026-06-20): per-customer Cloudflare Pages project created + deployed to via Wrangler direct upload (no GitHub needed for this simple path); credentials read through the audited config seam. Each customer gets a real siteagent-<slug>.pages.dev URL.

Pending: the full architecture target — production (Pages) + preview (Workers SSR) surfaces provisioned by provisionTenant(), env wiring, and the Cloudflare Access gate.

m9-publish-last.notes:
Go-live ordering.

Done (2026-06-20): a SIMPLE publish slice works — freeze draft (published version) → materialize to a static site (HTML + copied images) → deploy to Cloudflare → save the live URL. Proves the materialize→deploy→publish loop end-to-end.

Pending: the production-grade Module 9 saga — protected merge on the head SHA, single git-triggered build, deploy-verify, publish-via-Payload-API LAST, and all compensation/rollback states (aborted / failed_deploy_reverted / deployed_pending_publish). The current slice has no rollback and uploads directly rather than via a reviewed merge.

m14-collection.notes: ConnectedSites collection (tenant-scoped) holding site info + the built HTML + draft/published/previous content snapshots. Migrated. The draft/published/previous split is what makes the live site never break + one-click rollback.

m14-connect.notes: Connect a whole built site (dist folder or repo) — all pages + assets into Payload + managed folder. (Was single-URL; upgraded to whole-site.)

m14-html-engine.notes: The content engine: finds editable text/images in built HTML (data-sa markers + auto-detect of plain text/images) and applies edits back onto the same HTML. Verified: edits text+image, keeps the design/CSS/footer intact.

m14-edit.notes: Verified on a real site (2026-06-23): edit text by click (piece-by-piece, in-place, styling preserved) + AI chat (with reference image + shimmer skeleton reply), swap images, edit-mode toggle, undo, shared footer/nav/logo sync across pages (auto-refreshes other pages). data-sa markers fully supported (every piece). ⋮ options badge sits outside each text block. Content in Payload; code untouched.

m14-publish.notes: Verified end-to-end (2026-06-23): publish redeploys the whole built site to the client exact Cloudflare project (same URL); changes confirmed on the live URL; one-click rollback. Publish output byte-identical to the built HTML. Now runs as a tracked background JOB with a live progress bar (stage %, one-line logs, Cancel-with-cleanup) that survives page refresh + server restart (durable Jobs collection + in-memory registry + stale-job reaping). Code: src/jobs/*, collections/Jobs.ts, ProgressModal.

m14-rules.notes: docs/rules.md written — the build standard a client follows so a site is editable (recommended stack Astro static -> Cloudflare Pages; the golden rule = content in the built HTML; optional data-sa markers; images; handover checklist; example).

m14-multipage.notes: Whole-site connect (dist folder OR GitHub repo, cloned+built). All pages+assets; multi-page preview (crossfade); page tabs immediate on connect; body-only walk (no doctype leak).

m14-structural.notes: Add/remove sections & pages + design changes (via code) — a later phase, kept separate from content editing per AgentPlan.
